subject: ap-chemistry
title: Oxalate Ion & Silver Oxalate Solubility
stimulus: A Lewis electron-dot diagram of the oxalate ion, $\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$, is shown.

parts.prompt: Identify the hybridization of the valence orbitals of either carbon atom in the oxalate ion.
parts.scoring_notes: In the oxalate ion $\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$, each carbon is bonded to two oxygen atoms (one double bond C=O and one single bond C–O) and to one other carbon (C–C single bond) — a total of three electron domains. Three electron domains around carbon → $sp^2$ hybridization.

alternatives.correct_example: $sp^2$. Each carbon in $\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$ has three electron domains (one C=O double bond, one C–O single bond, and one C–C single bond). Three electron domains → $sp^2$ hybridization, with the unhybridized $p$ orbital participating in the $\pi$ bond with oxygen.

wrong_examples: Saying $sp^3$ — that would require four sigma domains (tetrahedral); carbon here has only three groups/domains.
Saying $sp$ — that would require only two groups (linear geometry); carbon here has three groups.
common_traps: Hybridization = number of sigma bonds + lone pairs on the central atom. Each carbon: 1 C–C + 1 C–O (single) + 1 C=O (counts as 1 sigma domain) = 3 sigma domains → $sp^2$. The $\pi$ bond uses the unhybridized $p$ orbital.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct — each carbon has three sigma domains (C–C, C–O single, C=O); matches SG.
Incorrect — $sp^3$ requires four sigma domains; each carbon here has only three.

parts.prompt:
Silver oxalate, $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4(s)$, is slightly soluble in water. The value of $K_{sp}$ for $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$ is $5.40 \times 10^{-12}$.

(i) Write the expression for the solubility-product constant, $K_{sp}$, for $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$.

(ii) Calculate the molar solubility of $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$ in neutral distilled water.

(iii) The molar solubility of $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$ increases when it is dissolved in 0.5 M $\text{HClO}_4(aq)$ instead of neutral distilled water. Write a balanced, net-ionic equation for the process that occurs between species in solution that contributes to the increased solubility of $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4(aq)$ in $\text{HClO}_4(aq)$.
parts.scoring_notes: (i) $K_{sp} = [\text{Ag}^+]^2[\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}]$. (ii) Let $s$ = molar solubility. $[\text{Ag}^+] = 2s$, $[\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}] = s$. $K_{sp} = (2s)^2(s) = 4s^3 = 5.40 \times 10^{-12}$. $s^3 = 1.35 \times 10^{-12}$. $s = (1.35 \times 10^{-12})^{1/3} = 1.11 \times 10^{-4}$ M. (iii) The oxalate ion is the conjugate base of a weak acid ($\text{H}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$). In acidic solution, $\text{H}^+$ reacts with $\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$ to form $\text{HC}_2\text{O}_4^-$ (or $\text{H}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$), removing oxalate from solution and driving more $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$ to dissolve. Net ionic equation: $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{HC}_2\text{O}_4^-(aq)$.

wrong_examples: Writing $K_{sp} = [\text{Ag}^+][\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}]$ without squaring $[\text{Ag}^+]$ — two $\text{Ag}^+$ ions are released per formula unit.
Including the solid in the expression — pure solids are omitted from equilibrium expressions.
common_traps: $K_{sp} = \prod [\text{ions}]^{\text{stoichiometric coefficient}}$. $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{Ag}^+ + \text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$. The 2 coefficient on $\text{Ag}^+$ becomes the exponent: $[\text{Ag}^+]^2$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated molar solubility: 1 point
wrong_examples: Setting $s^2 = K_{sp}/2$ — must use the full expression $(2s)^2(s) = 4s^3$, not simplify incorrectly.
Using $K_{sp} = s^2$ (1:1 stoichiometry) — $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$ gives 2 $\text{Ag}^+$ per formula unit.

scoring_points.description: For a correct balanced net-ionic equation showing protonation of oxalate by $\text{H}^+$: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{HC}_2\text{O}_4^-(aq)$

In $\text{HClO}_4(aq)$, the $\text{H}^+$ protonates $\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$ ions, forming the weaker acid $\text{HC}_2\text{O}_4^-$. This removes $\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$ from solution, driving the dissolution equilibrium of $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$ to the right and increasing solubility.

wrong_examples: Writing $\text{Ag}^+ + \text{ClO}_4^- \rightarrow \text{AgClO}_4$ — $\text{AgClO}_4$ is soluble; this reaction does not explain the increased solubility of oxalate.
Including the dissolution of $\text{Ag}_2\text{C}_2\text{O}_4$ in the net ionic equation — the question asks for the process in solution that drives the increased solubility.
common_traps: Increased solubility in acid = the anion of a slightly soluble salt is the conjugate base of a weak acid and is protonated by $\text{H}^+$. $\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$ (from weak diprotic oxalic acid) reacts with $\text{H}^+$ to form $\text{HC}_2\text{O}_4^-$, removing oxalate from solution and pulling more solid into solution.
